speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, with Zipora Nutovits. OK, you were saying? The elderly couple who knew your family, did they take you and hide you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't tell this story for nobody. If, if I want to tell you, this is the first time that I tell this story. Why I don't want to tell? Because when I went to [NON-ENGLISH], they told me, this is from Hungary, and this is not our issue, that I am behind. But I can tell you the story, what happened. The story is very-- I think this is a very special story. It was '43 when my mother heard that in Yugoslavia, they, they, they killed my sisters and my brothers-- murdered all the family. And he came there, the [NON-ENGLISH] tell me-- told my mother that something is going to happen. They told her they want to take me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I start to tell you a little bit, that they told me, for my mother, that they have a very special place on the roof. They have a-- they have a little like a room. And they can put me over there. But I must be very quiet and very nice. And I cannot run outside. And I cannot go to my home. And I need to be separate from my home and from my family. So first, my mother don't tell me what's going on, because when I came to them, every time I was free. You know , I was-- they have a very nice home. They have a lot of guests, all the time. And she, she had a lot of guests, you know, and they came and go, you know. Suddenly, my mother took me up with her-- with the woman-- and told me that I [BACKGROUND NOISE] can be over there. But nobody can-- nobody going to do-- nobody can know what, what-- I cannot be free. I need to be upstairs all the time. So I was very sad. I tried to cry. I told my mother, I don't want to leave my family. I want to be with you, whatever is going to be. I want to be with you. I don't want to be separate. I was, I don't know, about 10-- I was nine-- I don't remember. So one day I was over there, a holiday. Nobody saw, nobody know. And in the night, I came home again. This was about three, four days. And later, they come to me and speak with my mother, and they told me that I cannot come to home more. I need to decide, and they need to decide that I need to stay over there. So they make me a little hole-- a room-- over there, a little bed. And I have all kind of little-- Clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Closes. And I-- I start to be over there. The woman come up to me every time-- sometimes in the morning, sometimes in the middle of the day. They bring me food all the time, and to drink, and everything. But I was feeling very lonely. I was very sad. I was crying a lot. And I was feeling every time, very lonely and afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there-- why I need to be I, a special, that I need to be alone? And to separate from my family? The family is living near me, and I cannot see them because it was a roof that it was-- without light. It was alight only with little this light.

speaker: interviewee
text: She bring me all kind of food. She bring me all kind of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] little books. I can read in this time. I don't know, I can read. She bring me to write. And I write. But was very, very bad situation. I was feeling very, very bad. What happened, they have a very-- a farm. They have a farm. They was very rich people. They have a big farm. And because I don't feel good, they take me to the farm, outside. But over there, it was another situation. It was field, big fields. And the people came in the morning to work. And after noon, they left, because over there in the field, they working from 4:00 in the morning. Because it's very hot, sometimes, and they finish after noon.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Hungarian people, they was working in the field, the camp. And over there was a little house. And it was only a man, a old man that take care of everything. And when I came the first time, they told me who I am. And this man need to take care of me. But I was very lonely, again, because I can't take-- I can't-- I can't go outside in the day. Because the people was over there. They was working over there. And they know that they don't have children. So what I am doing over there? And the old woman-- the old man was lonely, too. He take care of the people over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the night, I can be outside. But I was afraid, because this is a big field, and you cannot see nothing. And it is very dark. You can see only the stars. And the stars is so hot, so shiny, that you almost can touch them, because is everything dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so over there a few weeks, because they want to me have a little time out and to be free, free, you know? But it was very bad situation, because the old man was talking for himself. He was drinking. And I was all the time afraid that-- she-- you know, I was a little girl alone with him in the other room. It was two rooms. I have another room. Even I clothe myself-- I clothe, I can clothe myself. I cannot be over there. I was afraid. So the [? gentle ?] come all the time, to see what going on. Hey, after two, three, three days, he came to see-- because the people was working over there. And I told I, I, I cannot be afraid. I afraid. So they take me again, over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back, I feel a little better. Because I don't afraid from the old man. And I feel a little better that I can-- I am near to my family. So this is went through about until my-- my family moved to, to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is almost a year. And it was terrible, terrible, terrible-- a very bad situation. I was sad. I was crying. I cannot eat. And the woman was very nice to me. She bring me food, and to drink, and to do everything, because she loved me. She want to save me. And I told her, you make me a very nice job, but I love my mother. I love my sisters. And want-- And they told, listen, Gizella-- she spoke with me in Hungarian. We try to save you. When everything is go over, you can go to back to your mommy. But it is-- you know, for a child to be alone all the time, with only your mind, and to-- to get-- only you know that your mommy's over there, five minute from me. And I cannot go. I cannot go, because-- and the people was free in this time, in '43. You know, without ghetto yet. But I was in ghetto, you know. I was not-- I was a prisoner-- of like a [NON-ENGLISH]. And before that, almost in the-- when we moved to the ghetto, the old man tried to be very sick. The [? gentle, ?] the [? gentle ?] where I was over there, he was very sick. And the woman was afraid that what can happen to me. So when, when they moved to the ghetto, to Hajdunanas, my mother took me with them. So this is my story, a very short story. So this story is very long for me. And I remember for all my life, that I was alone, and alone. And later, when I finish, I tell you that I never live alone. I never-- I cannot be in-- I cannot be in the elevator. I cannot be alone. And if somebody close me, I cannot be alone. I never sleeped without light. I can be five minute or 10 minute in home, but if somebody's not here, I open the door and I going out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if I-- if I-- you know, sometimes I am very tired. I sleep five minute on the sofa, you know. I don't sleep. I sleep like this. After five minute, I all time-- where is my sister? Where are my husband? Where is my children? I looking for them, all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: If my children going somewhere, and they don't come in the time, I am nervous. Or my, my husband is going somewhere, I am nervous right away. If I am going to doctor or somewhere, I never go alone. I going with my husband, all the time. Because I am not feeling good. I am not so healthy. I almost, when I spoke with you-- I have headache. I almost-- I feel very bad. I look like very nice, and I look like very healthy. But not so on inside. I am, oh, I am normal. I am [INAUDIBLE]. I am all right. I am acting very nice. But sometimes in the dream, I dreaming all times that I am alone. And something happening, and something missing, something take from me the best, good things that I have. So every time I, [CRYING] I looking for my children. You know, I have a grandchild. I love him very much. He is a very nice boy. He is a very lot-- a lot of energy. He make me a lot of problem . He live upstairs, and he has two houses. He told three, because my daughter is separate. And my grandchild sometimes going to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: His father.

speaker: interviewee
text: To that. His father. So he make a picture-- a big picture, this is ours, and the little picture, my, my daughter. And a little picture picture in the side, this is the father. So he make a lot of problem. And I love him very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he jumping, and coming, and make messes, and I all time tell-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And I give him all the time, you know, direction. Don't do that. Don't, don't jump on the sofa. But he's in the camp. I told my daughter, 5 o'clock? He's in summer camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, summer camp. At 5 o'clock, I, I suddenly I very anxious. He need to come to home. You know, I feel something strange. Even he make me trouble, I love him very much and everything. But you know-- and oh, something else-- when they going-- when my children went to, you know, to the-- went to the school, and you know, they take-- they go to the trip, I went with them all the time. No, the little one, the little one, there is a [INAUDIBLE] going to the camp, you know, and take them one a week to the beach, and sometimes they take to the movie, or something. I am so anxious. I am-- I am not, I am not quiet. I have, I have very bad feelings that-- [NON-ENGLISH]. Something, you know, can happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every time-- the last time he went some-- a trip, I went with my husband until the bus is moving. And in 5 o'clock, I was over there to see that he's coming to back. You understand? I have from childhood a very bad feeling that I feel that I am, I am lonely. If we could go back to that period, now, when you were reunited with your mother and the rest of the children, where did the family go from that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: So when--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went to the, to the ghetto together? We went to Hajdunanas in this day. They make a few street. I don't know, exactly. We was over there. It was very sticky, a lot of children. It was a lot of problem, crying. But you know, I was even-- it was very bad. I was happy-- more like over there. Because I was over-- I feel that I am with, with, with my family, and with my mother. So even, I was hungry, and I was don't feel good, and we don't have money, nothing, and not food and you know, but I more-- I was happy, because I was with my mother. I hold her all the time. I take her, and I hold her all the time. I want to feel that she's right. She's, she's with me. We was in Hajdunanas I don't know how much or how long. I know in March we moved to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: In May of 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in March, I think. In March.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, March?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they took-- I told you that we, we went, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well tell me again.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the ghetto to the train. We made to take a long, long trip. We walk, and the people were so quiet. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quiet. You went like, like, I don't know-- like, like not horses. The horses, there's a lot of-- they are, they are moving. Like the-- [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Cows. Cows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Cows.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since they are going, you know, they [INAUDIBLE] and going slowly, slowly. And everybody was quiet. Only the little children was crying, because they went a lot of-- [INAUDIBLE]. We came to the train. They put me-- they put us in the train, without money, without food, without drink. We came to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you prepare? Did you know in advance that you were going to be leaving the ghetto for Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know nothing. We don't know. We, we take-- we come to the train, where they put me-- they put us in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you bring with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We bring us-- I told you. Everybody-- my mother give for everybody a little packalet, a little package to hold. So everybody hold a little package. My mother hold two packages. And the children, everybody hold, you know, some clothes, some-- I don't know, every-- and food, and everything, whatever left-- and pictures, and all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my, my-- we came. And they took us from the, from the train, and they put us a a barrack, a big barrack. You see, a barrack, not a house, a big barrack. It was Judenhaus, Judenhaus in Hungarian, in-- in Budapest. And they put us together, a lot of people. And they brought us-- not from Hajdunanas. And other places, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came, and it was, you know, a lot of people with children-- and not-- not-- not mens, not mens-- only a little mens. They took the mens for work, for the work. Only the mens-- the-- only old mens was over there. But, but a lot of people was over there. Peoples, womens, mothers and children. And it was a big [NON-ENGLISH], a lot of people, you know, with packlets. We don't know what to do. And they start to-- after-- I don't know if it's one day or two days. I don't know, exactly. They put us in ghetto. I know that I was in [NON-ENGLISH]. This I remember. And over there, I don't know if we have two rooms, one rooms, I don't know. I don't know. I don't remember. I don't want-- I don't know. I know it start to be very bad situation. I know everybody has a star. And we-- and I know that everybody can go outside for one or two hours to buy something. And I, I, I I don't remember, but somebody told us that because when you have money, you can buy. But if you know that you are Jew, they don't want to sell for you food, even if you had money. Or sometimes, they, they ask you for more money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happen, one day a man want to go outside. He take off the star. And somebody saw him. And he disappeared. What happened? We don't know. So I know that I was a few-- I don't know how long I was in Budapest in ghetto, but I was very sick. And no, no-- over there was doctors too, but no doctors, you know, and not medicine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I get very bad diarrhea. And some woman told me that I have some-- I can help. So she took me a few-- again, I was separate from my, from my family, again. And I was-- I don't know how long. After that, I came back. We was over there a year, a year with a very bad situation. And before when we finish, when we finish, we're liberated, I was very sick. And right away, somebody took me to the hospital. So I was, again, separate from my, from my family. I think I was-- after I-- I think we was-- we was in January or February was free. And they come. The Russian people came to us, in Hungary-- not from America. And I know that they took me, right away, to hospital, because I was very bad situation. I have diarrhea, and I have with the urine, something very bad. And I suffered a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were, before that, with your mother and all of your sisters and brother. How did you get your food? How did your family get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad s-- I told you, before we go to the ghetto, my mother prepared a lot of food. So when we came to, to-- because I don't know how long we was in ghetto, in Hajdunanas. But when we came to Budapest, we left a little food. So my mother save and give, give for us. But we was hungry all the time-- hungry all the time. In the night, we cannot sleep. We was afraid. We was afraid, very afraid. And, and we was hungry all the time. So when I was, when I was-- when we, when we finish, when we was free, I was very sick. And took me to the hospital. And when I was-- I think I was-- I don't know how long, in the hospital. At the hospital, they take me to a special place where is only children-- a lot of children from Hungary. They collect a lot of children, because they have lots of children without parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother-- I know that my mother and sisters was in ghetto, [NON-ENGLISH]. You understand? So I know that they are over there, and we are free. But I was very sick. I get all kind of medicine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, when I get a little better, they took me-- together with a lot of children. We was in Budapest, I don't know how long, because everything-- it was very short time. And we, we have, over there, teachers take care of us, and start to be not teach us-- only, you know, to give us music, and to dance with us, to give us very good food, to tell us stories, because we was very young. But it was all kind of ages. It was more-- it was children. It was more bigger, like me. But they was very little and skinny, so nobody can know what is their ages. So they collect us, and they collect from all the world ten hundred-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: --together. And they take us to German, to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Without the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but they know that I left. They know that I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family knew you were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they know. They know-- of course, this is, this is free. And they told us that they-- we came to [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was very-- you know, it was, is a very bad situation, because the train is not working at this time. So they take us with, with, with big cars, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: --trucks. They put us-- and we, we brought-- they took us together, and bring us to [NON-ENGLISH] In [NON-ENGLISH]-- no, in, in-- not in [NON-ENGLISH]-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Vienna, Vienna-- we came to Vienna. They put us in a big, big, very nice house with a lot of children. But the children was-- it was all kind of children from all over the world. And there, there was a lot of children sick. They, they were sick, sick like-- not sick, that you are sick, diarrhea. They're sick that you can't get the-- [NON-ENGLISH]. You can't be sick with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Contagious.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were contagious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they, they take-- took us-- it was about three levels, a big, big house. So they took us another place, because we was healthy. And they hold us there, a few days, again. Later, I go to Pocking. I was in Pocking. And over there I was. And they teach us a little Yiddish, because it was from Poland, from Romania, from Hungary. And they don't know what kind of language they make us at school. And the little children start to go to the school. And they start to teach us Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- what organization was this, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know what is--

speaker: interviewer
text: An Israeli organization? A Zionist organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is not-- this is not from Israel. This is from all the world, from all the world, They send money. And they want to take the children and to give them something, you know. And they, they decide that they're going to teach us Yiddish. I understand everything Yiddish, but this was very difficult. I don't know how to read. I don't know how to write. It was a very short time that I was over there. And they decide that these children, they want to send all over the world. So they came. We take-- I was in Pocking I don't know exactly how long. I don't know. And we came to [NON-ENGLISH], to German. And we put again-- they put together, us. And they come from Israel, the people. They come from Schweitz, Schwyt, Swede. They come from United States. And they want to take us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they make us interview, like a children. And they, they ask, what is your name? What is your age? Who are you? From where are you? What do you want to do? You want to go to Israel? You want to go to Swede, to Schweitz? You want to go to England? You want to go to New York, to United States? Whatever you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they ask from where are you, if I am religious or not. I told yes, I am religious. And I want to go only to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you write down. And some people came again. It was three, four days. And you know, I was a little child. Nobody-- you know, nobody came-- I was only like-- no, no, with-- I sit, myself. Nobody was with me, you know. Only I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, they tried to tell me that the United States is very nice. You can go to some family. You can go to institute. You can take some-- somebody can teach you. Again, from Swede and from England-- you know, just came people. And there, I had done everything what I told. And I told again, three or four times, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think because we have people from Israel, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to break, and then we'll start a new tape.
